title: Quick reframes for tough moments
lines: Fear is a normal signal—it means you care about safety. Use it to act step by step.
Tiredness makes problems look bigger. Rest 20 minutes, then re-check calmly.
Loneliness at sea is real, but every log you write connects you to sailors before you.

title: Simple routines to keep balance
lines: Set a 20-minute cycle: check surroundings → act → write a short log → rest.
Eat small bites and sip water regularly to keep energy steady.
Stretch arms and legs every hour to reduce stiffness and fatigue.

title: Remember you are an explorer
lines: Your notes matter—small details become part of the ocean’s story.
Each observation you log is like a step on unknown land.
You are not just surviving; you are exploring.

title: Glowing water
mission: After dark, dim lights and look leeward for glow.
why: Glow usually comes from plankton. Recording it helps understand sea currents.

title: Upwelling basics
fact: Sometimes cooler water rises from deep below, bringing nutrients.
mission: Notice if water feels colder than usual.
Look for sea birds or fish gathering in one spot.
why: These signs may mean nutrient-rich waters below.

title: Tides and rip signs
fact: Strong surface flows can form where water moves quickly back out to sea.
mission: If safe, watch foam or floating objects—they may move faster in narrow lines.
Note the time and sea state in your log.
why: Tracking helps understand local water movement patterns.

title: Sky and cloud clues
fact: High wispy clouds can come before wind changes; layered gray clouds can bring steady rain.
mission: Look up every hour and sketch cloud shapes in your log.
why: Simple observations help anticipate weather changes.

title: Birds and fish signs
fact: Seabirds circling or diving often show where small fish gather.
mission: Log direction and type of bird activity.
Note water color or debris lines nearby.
why: Birds can signal currents or areas rich in life.

title: Micro observation missions
missions: Check horizon every 20 minutes and note wave direction.
Drop a small float (bottle, stick) and time its drift for 5 minutes.
Record sea color at sunrise and sunset.
why: Small repeated notes turn into valuable patterns.

title: Folklore short notes
snippets: Bird flocks at sea were seen as guides toward land.
Ancient logs turned small observations into navigation wisdom.

title: Immediate safety basics
steps: Wear your life jacket; clip in if possible.
Secure loose gear and close hatches.
Slow down; keep bow angled into waves, not side-on.
Take a sip of water and a bite to keep focus.

title: Basics for heavy weather
steps: Steer to meet waves at a slight angle (20–30° off the bow).
Avoid letting waves hit directly from the side.
If overwhelmed, slow further and hold steady course.

title: Distress basics
steps: If you have radio: give name, position, people aboard, type of problem.
Use visible signals (bright cloth, light, sound) if available.
Make signals early; waiting reduces chance of help.

title: Keep orientation with a log
steps: Write time, heading (approx), wind, sea state every hour.
Note unusual sounds or smells.
Sketch rough drift of floating objects if you can.

title: Cold and heat basics
steps: Cold: get dry, add layers, cover head and core.
Heat: shade if possible, sip water, loosen clothing.
why: Protecting body temperature prevents fatigue and confusion.

title: First aid simple steps
steps: For bleeding: apply firm pressure with cloth.
For seasickness: fresh air, small sips of water.
For dizziness: sit, breathe slow, rest.
notes: Keep steps short and repeatable. Seek medical help if possible.

title: Night operations and fatigue
steps: Use dim red light if available; keep one light on deck.
Share watch if more than one person; swap every 20–30 minutes.
If alone: set alarm for micro-rests (15–20 minutes).

title: Mental first aid
steps: Stop and breathe slowly 3 times.
Say aloud one small step you will do now (e.g., check gear).
Write one line in the logbook to anchor yourself.

title: Glossary of simple terms
entries: Drift: slow movement of vessel due to wind/current.
